issue_title: Unexpected error message when incorrect subcmd supplied
issue_body:
## Issue

When an incorrect subcommand is supplied, the error says that there were `unexpected positional arguments`.

Based on my understanding, positional arguments are not allowed at levels in which there exist further subcommands. So, I believe the error should say something along the lines of `incorrect subcommand provided`.

## Reproduce

```python
from face import Command

cmd = Command(lambda: print("foo"), "foo")
subcmd = Command(lambda: print("bar"), "bar")
subcmd.add(Command(lambda: print("baz"), "baz"))
cmd.add(subcmd)

cmd.run(argv=["foo", "bar", "ba"])  # ba should be baz
```

Results in the following error:

```
error: foo: unexpected positional arguments: ['ba']
```

---

If you agree with the above, let me know and I can make it happen.

issue_title: Implement a plugin mechanism for custom Python scripts
issue_body:
Implement a plugin system in foliadocserve and FLAT, that allows custom Python scripts to be executed in the context of the foliadocserve. This would add a lot of flexibility and allow custom functionality to be added.

The setup would be as follows:
* The plugin is declared in the FLAT configuration, this specifies 1) the python module to import, 2) the function to call in said module, 3) the user parameters the function accepts. 4) A label for a menu entry
* A plugin script has to be rewritten and should define an entry function adhering to as specific signature, something like ``f(namespace,docid, doc, **kwargs)`` where ``doc`` is an instance of ``folia.Document`` and allows full read+write access to the document. The output of the function is either 1) an FQL query (possibly with some extra parameters) that tells FLAT what to visualize or 2) an HTML snippet that will be shown in a separate response dialog.
* From a user perspective, the user selects an item tied to a plugin in the menu, if any user defined parameters are taken, a dialog pops up to query those. Upon submission of this dialog (or direct click if no parameters are needed), FLAT communicates to foliadocserve to invoke the plugin with said parameters.

Requested by @ErkanBasar

issue_title: How to fully qualify statement data type?
issue_body:
Looking to retrieve the fully qualified data type of a statement, is there a correct way to go about doing this? Current methodology:

```python
def get_type(node):
    """Gets the immediate, top-level type of the node.
    """
    type_obj = node.search_one('type')
    fq_type_name = None
    if type_obj is not None:
        type_module = type_obj.i_orig_module.arg
        type_name = type_obj.arg
        fq_type_name = '%s:%s' % (type_module, type_name)
    return fq_type_name
```
EDIT: Repo now at https://github.com/remingtonc/pyang-as-lib

Which produces potentially desired output such as:
* `"Cisco-IOS-XR-ipv4-bgp-oper-sub1:Ipv4-flowspec-address"`
However there are cases of potentially undesirable output which appear to already be qualified before addition of the module name such as:
* `"Cisco-IOS-XR-ipv4-bgp-oper-sub1:inet:ipv4-address"`
* `"Cisco-IOS-XR-ipv4-bgp-oper-sub1:yang:hex-string"`

How would you go about fully qualifying a data type? Is the current methodology/output format correct?

issue_title: Allow a list of valid audiences to be configured
issue_body:
A JWT token can contain multiple audiences.

But, it would be nice if you could also specify a list of valid audiences for `audience` in `jwt.decode()`.

Example:

``` Python
options = {
    'verify_signature': True,
    'verify_exp': True,
    'verify_nbf': True,
    'verify_iat': True,
    'verify_aud': True,
    'verify_iss': True,
    'require_exp': False,
    'require_iat': False,
    'require_nbf': False
}

payload = jwt.decode(
    jwt_value,
    key=self._public_key,
    verify=True,
    audience=["audience_1", "audience_2"],
    issuer="SomeIssuer",
    options=options,
)
```

issue_title: Add configuration flag to assert all requests were played
issue_body:
It would be nice to be able to configure VCR to assert that all recorded requests must be played without having to write the assertion explicitly.

The idea is to be able to ensure that all the requests you recorded are indeed being called by the code being exercised by a test: to avoid cases where a test will pass even if the code doesn't do a request that was expected but doesn't have any "assertable" results.

Here is a pattern I've been using in some test code:

    with vcr.use_cassette('some_cassette'):
        ... exercise code here
        self.assertTrue(cassette.dirty or cassette.all_played, "Expected more requests")

It would be nice to be able to do:

    @vcr.use_cassette(require_all_played=True)
    def test_something
        ... exercise code here

issue_title: Name field sometimes None on Windows
issue_body:
Sometimes, for some odd reason, the psutil returns a process with a name of `None`.

For instance, using this code:
```python
import psutil
import time

def fetchProcesses():
	for p in psutil.process_iter():
		try:
			p.dict = p.as_dict(['pid', 'name', 'cpu_percent'])
			
			if p.dict['cpu_percent'] > 70:
				print p.dict
		except psutil.NoSuchProcess:
			pass
fetchProcesses()
time.sleep(0.5)
fetchProcesses()
```

I'll get output like this:
```
{'name': u'PlexScriptHost.exe', 'cpu_percent': 97.4}
{'name': u'PlexDlnaServer.exe', 'cpu_percent': 97.4}
{'name': None, 'cpu_percent': 85.2}
```

So I then tried playing with win32api a bit to grab the file name:

```python
def fetchProcesses():
	for p in psutil.process_iter():
		try:
			p.dict = p.as_dict(['pid', 'name', 'cpu_percent'])
			
			if p.dict['cpu_percent'] > 70:
				if p.dict['name'] == None:
					print "DEBUG: need to correct process name"
					print "DEBUG: opening PID: %i" % p.dict['pid']
					pHandle = win32api.OpenProcess(
						win32con.PROCESS_VM_READ | win32con.PROCESS_QUERY_INFORMATION,
						0, p.dict['pid'])
					if pHandle:
						filen = win32process.GetModuleFileNameEx(pHandle, 0)
						print "Got:", filen
```

Which resulted in this error:

```
{'pid': 7272, 'name': u'PlexScriptHost.exe', 'cpu_percent': 92.3}
{'pid': 9360, 'name': u'PlexDlnaServer.exe', 'cpu_percent': 96.5}
DEBUG: need to correct process name
DEBUG: opening PID: 23320
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "monitor.py", line 27, in <module>
    fetchProcesses()
  File "monitor.py", line 17, in fetchProcesses
    0, p.dict['pid'])
pywintypes.error: (5, 'OpenProcess', 'Access is denied.')
```

Supposedly, you can do some Windows magic to ask for special privileges for making OpenProcess work, but it seemed a bit too hacky at the time, so I avoided it. (You may opt to try this in the future, though! `OpenProcessToken` is the starting point.)

I took a cursory look at your code, and it looks like you use OpenProcess as well for process information. (I might be wrong about how you use it... feel free to correct me!)

To work around the issue, I wrote this:
```python
import win32com  
from win32com.client import GetObject  
WMI = GetObject('winmgmts:')
processes = WMI.InstancesOf('Win32_Process')

for process in processes:
	try:
		print('PID: ', process.Properties_('ProcessID').Value, 'process: ', process.Properties_('Name').Value)
	except:
		pass
```

This does the trick for me, yielding:
```
...
('process: ', u'blender-app.exe', 'PID: ', 23320)
...
```

For now, using the above code will be a workaround in case this happens.

It looks like using WMI might be an interesting vector to try. (It might even potentially fix #549... maybe.) What do you think?

issue_title: Bot doesn't execute function or exits with code 0
issue_body:
To summarize, when a bot admin uses !update command, it starts the update chain.

1. Gets a member list and server roles
2. Sees if any of the members already have any region role, removes them from the list if they do (to avoid making unnecessary queries)
3. Checks if there's someone with their nick/name in the regions, then returns a dict. With a key/value of nick/name : list_of_regions
4. If a dict is returned (the members list could be empty), it starts setting roles 
5. Finds the nick/name in the dictionary and adds role to the member with the respective nick/name.

However, it stops working just after entering step 4. Sometimes it exits the bot with exit code 0, others the bot keeps running, but without reaching doing anything in step 4.

The functions for step 4 and 5:
```python
async def roles(user, future_roles, server_roles):
    """
    :param user - A Member that is going to be assigned a role
    :param future_roles - A list of Names that match existing Roles.names
    :param server_roles - Self explanatory
    """
    print("you have reached roles function")
    for role in server_roles:
        if role.name in future_roles:
            await  client.add_roles(user, role)
            print("Role {} added to {}".format(role.name, user.name))


async def set_roles(user_region_dict, list_users, server_roles):
    """
    :param user_region_dict - A dictionary with Keys being Member's nicks or names, values the regions where they play
    :param list_users: - Self explanatory
    :param server_roles - Self explanatory

    How it works:
                Checks each member.nick/member.name in the dictionary and assigns the respective member
                that is in list_users due to the index variable.

                It calls the function roles with future_roles param being the region(s) where they play
    """
    print("You've reached set roles")
    for index, member in enumerate(list_users):
        if member.nick is not None:
            if member.nick in user_region_dict:
                print("Setting roles to {}".format(member.nick))
                await roles(list_users[index], user_region_dict[member.nick], server_roles)
        elif member.name in user_region_dict:
            print("Setting roles to {}".format(member.name))
            await roles(list_users[index], user_region_dict[member.name], server_roles)
```

Creating the issue because I'm not sure if it's my code that is wrong or if it's conflicting with the discord.py library.
I'm somewhat familiar with python but I probably make a lot of mistakes so feel free to call out on anything that I could improve ^^
[Picture to exemplify a test run](http://i.imgur.com/obv5NAr.png?1)
Note: The "Setting roles in a bit" is before entering step 4

issue_title: Memory leak in consumer when using lz4 compression
issue_body:
I've noticed a problem with unbounded memory usage in the consumer when handling lz4-compressed payloads. The symptoms point to a memory leak of some description. I've created a [test case](https://gist.github.com/declantraynor/88a1477c7430601f6e14af1c2998effe) which should allow easy reproduction of the issue. 

I've tried experimenting with various consumer settings such as `fetch_max_bytes `, `max_partition_fetch_bytes` and `max_in_flight_requests_per_connection` in an attempt to lessen the buffering requirements of each individual request to Kafka. In all cases, memory usage of consumer processes has continued to rise until such a point that those processes are killed by the host system.

I can additionally confirm that this issue is present in the latest PyPI release (1.3.2) as well as `master` and that the issue only manifests when using `lz4` compression specifically. `gzip` appears to be working fine, for example.

Any help is greatly appreciated. Thanks!

issue_title: HELP: kafka Consumer doesn't work with replication
issue_body:
Hi,
I've made 3 brokers which are
localhost:9092, localhost:9093, localhost:9094
and I have made a topic with a console:
$KAFKA_HOME/bin/kafka-topics.sh --create --zookeeper localhost:2181 --topic replica1 --replication-factor 3 --partition 3

The codes work fine when all servers are up, but when I force a 9092 server down, the consumer class start to not work while it works on console.
When I check the status with kafka-topics.sh --describe --zookeeper localhost:2181 --topic replica1, it shows that the leader has been changed and that the ISR has only 2 servers.

As I understand the Kafka, it automatically chnages the leader when the original server is down, and the consumer starts to read the message from the changed leader, which is why we are using replication-factor. Consumer consumes messages from the leader broker, and when the leader broker has unexpectedly disconnected, another broker becomes the leader, and consumer starts to consume messages from the different leader.
That's what I'm understanding about kafka replication.

I wanted to check which servers are in connection, so I tried to use kafka.conn.collect_hosts('localhost') and I expected it to show localhost:9093, localhost:9094 when I force localhost:9092 down, but it ONLY showed 'localhost:9092'.

What I have tried to fix the problem is giving only two bootstrap_servers when declaring consumer.

     try:

         bootstrap_servers.append(servers)
     except:
         logging.warning("Brokers not available")
consumer = KafkaConsumer(bootstrap_servers = bootstrap_servers, {other configs such as auto_offset_rest = 'earliest'})
consumer.subscribe('replica1')

for message in consumer:
      print message.value**

Even though replica1 has more than 70,000 messages in the lag, consumer doesn't show any messages...

Can anyone explain how my understanding is wrong?
Is there any way to fix this problem?

This ticket is a result of https://github.com/aio-libs/aiokafka/issues/176. The issue is that producer will raise UnknownTopicOrPartitionError to the user if a manual partition reassignment is in process.
How to reproduce:
* Create a multi-broker cluster, for ex. as here https://kafka.apache.org/quickstart#quickstart_multibroker. Say we have 3 nodes: `broker0`, `broker1`, `broker2`
* Set a producer that sends 1 message per second to the replicated topic
* Kill one of the nodes (say `broker2`) and launch another oner with a new config (new node `broker3`).
* Reassign partitions to the new node using instructions here https://kafka.apache.org/documentation/#basic_ops_automigrate
* You will get UnknownTopicOrPartitionError immediately on the producer.

As I understand `NotLeaderForPartition` is only raised if node actually contains replication data on it. On reassignment a previous Leader partition will migrate data to a new node and remove it's data, so the error raised will be UnknownTopicOrPartitionError instead.

Java client treats `UnknownTopicOrPartitionError` as retriable too:
https://kafka.apache.org/protocol#protocol_error_codes
https://github.com/apache/kafka/blob/0.11.0.0/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/errors/UnknownTopicOrPartitionException.java

Hi,

I just ran into an issue with the locale for simplified Chinese characters: zh_Hans. The zh_CN and zh_TW locales were deprecated in Django 1.9 (which is good). Trouble is that Django only accepts the .mo files if they are compiled when the locale folder is formatted as zh_Hans. Unfortunately, Rosetta does not recognize this format. The locale will only show up in the Rosetta UI if the locale folder is formatted as zh_HANS (note that with one only the first letter behind the underscore is capitalized and with the other, the entire part is capitalized).

Since I didn't know which formatting was the correct one (zh_Hans or zh_HANS), Claude from the Django i18n discussion group suggests zh_Hans is the correct formatting meaning that Rosetta might need updating in this regard.

https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/Django-I18N/o-H2DapWnvo

Looking at the current modules, I found that the option allowing users
to define a custom output format is not consistent or not present. One
thing that made me like i3status was the idea that no matter the module,
it is the `format` option that is used to customize its output. I
think it would be nice to uniformize existant modules around this idea
and to document this as the way to go for future modules.

Beside the uniformization, it offers more flexibility for users : take
for example a simple module like
[whoami](https://github.com/Ultrabug/py3status/blob/master/py3status/modules/whoami.py).
It actually only offers the possibility to display the username of the
user running py3status. No formatting of the output is possible.
Wouldn't it be nice if it defined a `format = "{username}"` in the
configuration ? It would allow the following :
- Still the same default behavior
- Definable by users in i3status.conf, to add simple things sur as
  prefix or suffix (which are definitely handy and pretty when used with
  UTF8 icons)
- By using directly `{username}` and making it available to the user,
  there isn't more parsing to do on the module side. Just return
  `format.format(username = username)` as response and it's done and
  working for both default value and possible user custom values

Details for existing modules (OK means it does it the way I described as
ideal; KO means the output is absolutely not configurable atm; links are
on the concerned lines) :
- [aws_bill](https://github.com/Horgix/py3status/blob/master/py3status/modules/aws_bill.py#L38)
  : KO; no output customization available
- [battery_level](https://github.com/Horgix/py3status/blob/master/py3status/modules/battery_level.py#L40)
  has a `format` option but with unamed parameters, which is probably
  not intuitive for users that would want to redefine it themselves
- [bitcoin_price](https://github.com/Horgix/py3status/blob/master/py3status/modules/bitcoin_price.py#L38)
  KO; doesn't have any output configuration
- [bluetooth](https://github.com/Horgix/py3status/blob/master/py3status/modules/bluetooth.py#L38)
  **OK**
- [clementine](https://github.com/Horgix/py3status/blob/master/py3status/modules/clementine.py#L16)
  KO; offers absolutely no output costomization, would probably greatly
  benefit from it and would be easily adaptable
- [dpms](https://github.com/Horgix/py3status/blob/master/py3status/modules/dpms.py#L16)
  : KO; no customization, easy to add
- [glpi](https://github.com/Horgix/py3status/blob/master/py3status/modules/glpi.py#L24)
  KO; would benefit from it
- [imap](https://github.com/Horgix/py3status/blob/master/py3status/modules/imap.py#L33)
  **OK**
- [keyboard_layout](https://github.com/Horgix/py3status/blob/master/py3status/modules/keyboard_layout.py#L54)
  KO; has no output config
- [mpd_status](https://github.com/Ultrabug/py3status/blob/master/py3status/modules/mpd_status.py#L47)
  **OK**
- [net_rate](https://github.com/Horgix/py3status/blob/master/py3status/modules/net_rate.py#L40)
  **OK**
- [netdata](https://github.com/Horgix/py3status/blob/master/py3status/modules/netdata.py#L49)
  doesn't have a configurable output but already uses .format strings
- [ns_checker](https://github.com/Horgix/py3status/blob/master/py3status/modules/ns_checker.py#L27)
  KO; satisfies itself by only displaying a defined output with "NOK" or
  "OK", and would benefit from this uniformization
- [nvidia_temp](https://github.com/Horgix/py3status/blob/master/py3status/modules/nvidia_temp.py#L35)
  offers customization of prefix and units (suffix) : would benefit from
  this standardization
- [online_status](https://github.com/Horgix/py3status/blob/master/py3status/modules/online_status.py#L29)
  allows output customization with `format_offline` and `format_online`
  as options. Having the `format_online` named `format` would be more
  intuitive and consistent
- [pingdom](https://github.com/Horgix/py3status/blob/master/py3status/modules/pingdom.py#L29)
  KO; doesn't allow any output customization and would benefit from a
  `format` option
- [player_control](https://github.com/Horgix/py3status/blob/master/py3status/modules/player_control.py#L40)
  already have customizable icons, but would probably be improved by a
  `format` for people who want to add a prefix such as an UTF8 music
  note or that kind of stuff
- [pomodoro](https://github.com/Horgix/py3status/blob/master/py3status/modules/pomodoro.py#L36)
  looks a bit tricky to adapt to this suggestion
- [rate_counter](https://github.com/Horgix/py3status/blob/master/py3status/modules/rate_counter.py#L20)
  KO; has a completely hardcoded output
- [scratchpad_counter](https://github.com/Horgix/py3status/blob/master/py3status/modules/scratchpad_counter.py#L29)
  uses a .format string and document what `{}` will be replaced for, but
  a named argument would make it uniformized with the other modules
- [screenshot](https://github.com/Horgix/py3status/blob/master/py3status/modules/screenshot.py#L31)
  KO but seems to have a really variable output depending on clicks,
  maybe not adaptable
- [spaceapi](https://github.com/Horgix/py3status/blob/master/py3status/modules/spaceapi.py#L33)
  has a partially customizable output, but would need a renaming. The
  `%H` instead of strings formatted with `{x}` is probably a limitation
  imposed by datetime
- [spotify](https://github.com/Horgix/py3status/blob/master/py3status/modules/spotify.py#L34)
  **OK**; does everything right
- [sysdata](https://github.com/Horgix/py3status/blob/master/py3status/modules/sysdata.py#L77)
  KO
- [vnstat](https://github.com/Horgix/py3status/blob/master/py3status/modules/vnstat.py#L65)
  **OK** : does it exactly as suggested
- [weather_yahoo](https://github.com/Horgix/py3status/blob/master/py3status/modules/weather_yahoo.py#L26)
  KO

After #45 it's become more apparent some things are a bit wonky.

The first one that comes to mind is that the major/backported keywords aren't consistent - a 'backported' feature or support item gets put in *both* bugfix releases *and* feature releases, but 'major' bugs are *only* considered "feature-like" and live in feature releases only.

Given the way we treat bugfix/tertiary releases - "feature releases are assumed to include prior bugfixes implicitly" - this seems incorrect. Either that assumption holds or it doesn't.

However changing this would be backwards incompatible (oh the irony) so we'd want there to be a 2.0 release with this sort of change.

Also a good time to more strongly do away with backported/major or e.g. make them distinct issue types of their own like `:major-bug:` or `:minor-feature` (though maybe better terms would be nice since that's confusing wrt major/minor release numbering...heh).

And of course, a good excuse to continue the internals cleanup started with #45.

Mouse buttons with number higher than 9 are indistinguishable from each other on XOrg implementaion of mouse `Listener`. That's of course caused by enum `Button` which doesn't support buttons higher than 9 and thus listener reports `Button.unknown`. 

I cannot see a reason for such limitation. Of course there is a workaround but I believe this should be changed anyway.

Workaround to detect button with number higher than 9 in listener:
```
from pynput import mouse

def number_aware_button(number):
    try:
        button = mouse.Button(number)
    except ValueError:
        button = mouse.Button.unknown
    button.button_number = number
    return button

def on_click(x, y, button, pressed):
    print('Button {0} {1} {2}'.format(
        button,
        button.button_number,
        'pressed' if pressed else 'released')
        )

with mouse.Listener(on_click=on_click) as listener:
    listener._button = number_aware_button
    listener.join()
```

    with open(join(STATIC_PATH, "index.html"), "r") as f:
        app["SWAGGER_TEMPLATE_CONTENT"] = (
            f.read()
            .replace("##SWAGGER_CONFIG##", '/{}{}'.
                     format(api_base_url.lstrip('/'), _swagger_def_url))
            .replace("##STATIC_PATH##", '/{}{}'.
                     format(api_base_url.lstrip('/'), statics_path))
            .replace("##SWAGGER_VALIDATOR_URL##", swagger_validator_url)

issue_title: Not handling "type: integer" without a corresponding "format:"
issue_body:
I am using pyswagger to execute an API call that returns an object with several integer properties.  The Swagger for each property specifies only "type: integer"; it does not specify a format (int32 or int64).

When I execute the API, pyswagger raises an exception:


      File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pyswagger/primitives/__init__.py", line 182, in produce
        val = _2nd(obj, ret, val, ctx)
      File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pyswagger/primitives/comm.py", line 40, in _2nd_pass_obj
        return ret.apply_with(obj, val, ctx)
      File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pyswagger/primitives/_model.py", line 29, in apply_with
        self[k] = ctx['factory'].produce(pobj, v)
      File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pyswagger/primitives/__init__.py", line 178, in produce
        raise ValueError('Can\'t resolve type from:(' + str(obj.type) + ', ' + str(obj.format) + ')')
    ValueError: Can't resolve type from:(integer, None)


I tracked this down to this code in the Primitives constructor (primitives/__init__.py):

        self._map = {
            # int
            'integer': {
                'int32': (create_int, validate_int),
                'int64': (create_int, validate_int),
            },


It seems that pyswagger requires a format, either 'int32' or 'int64', and fails when no format is specified.

 I solved my problem by adding a new entry to the map:

        self._map = {
            # int
            'integer': {
                'int32': (create_int, validate_int),
                'int64': (create_int, validate_int),
                None: (create_int, validate_int),
            },

It does not appear to me that the Swagger specification requires a "format" for an integer type.  In addition, the above code shows that pyswagger doesn't treat int32 and int64 differently. 

Is there a reason why pyswagger is insisting on a format for an integer type?

Thanks.

issue_title: _in_stoplist should return True for entities trimmed out of existence
issue_body:
In an entity like "-aromatic" which is in IGNORE_SUFFIX the resultant entity after running _in_stoplist is of length 0, hence the entity should be ignored (i.e. the function should return True) rather than reporting a 0 length entity.

On an entity which is both in IGNORE_PREFIX and IGNORE_SUFFIX you can get into a situation where the end index is actually before the start end index!
>>> d = Document("non-aromatic")
>>> d.cems
[Span(u'', 4, 3)]

I assume adding this check that the resultant entity's length is > 0 will fix that case as well.

I was skimming through the code and I noticed that the RecurrentNetwork activation is implemented in a rather strange way.

```py
for i, v in zip(self.input_nodes, inputs):
       ivalues[i] = v
       ovalues[i] = v

for node, activation, aggregation, bias, response, links in self.node_evals:
        node_inputs = [ivalues[i] * w for i, w in links]
        s = aggregation(node_inputs)
        ovalues[node] = activation(bias + response * s)
```

This means that any edge from a node that is not an input is naturally recurrent. In other words, all hidden nodes belong to a single layer with recurrent edges to the previous time-step (excluding the inputs); and thus we cannot create a multi-layer RNN. Is this behavior intentional?

issue_title: Commit cfedc42767f47a467407a562f086d7eba8cc76dd hides logs from junit xml report file when junitxml option is used.
issue_body:
When --junitxml option is used with pytest together with the pytest-catchlog plugin we used to see the Captured log after the failure/error message. 
Commit cfedc42767f47a467407a562f086d7eba8cc76dd breaks that, and hides the logs from that file.
I believe that the problem lies in the use of "item.add_report_section('call', 'log', log)" instead of "long_repr.addsection('Captured log', log)", since now we have a new report section created which is not parsed by the junitxml.py file.

I read the comments made in #10 about handling timezones and DST transitions. The solutions given is is inadequate, because parse-crontab, after calculating the future datetime, computes the timedelta between the future and the passed in now (tz-aware) datetime. 

So consider the following example: 
```
dt = datetime(2016, 03, 13, hour=5, minute=0, tzinfo=est) # where est is the America/New_York implementation
print CronTab("00 09 13 MAR *").next(dt)
```
The output is 4 hours, instead of 5 hours, because next() does the following \__sub\__ operation
```
future - dt # i.e. 2016-03-13 09:00:00-04:00 2016-03-13 05:00:00-05:00
```
That operation does return the expected delay because, according to the python docs:
> Subtraction of a datetime from a datetime is defined only if both operands are naive, or if both are aware. If one is aware and the other is naive, TypeError is raised.
If both are naive, or both are aware and have the same tzinfo attribute, the tzinfo attributes are ignored, and the result is a timedelta object t such that datetime2 + t == datetime1. No time zone adjustments are done in this case.

Which makes sense, because, I think, the intent was to have datetime arithmetic operations and timedelta objects have the Group property (i.e. behave similar to regular integers). If tzinfo attributes were considered when calling \__sub\__, then datetime2 + t would not equal datetime1. 
But I don't think parse-crontab behave like this, because the result of a next() isn't a timedelta, but the number of seconds left from _till the datetime passed in_.
Right now, i can get around this by adding the number of seconds to utcnow().replace(tzinfo-est), normalize the resulting future datetime, then compute the difference *again* to get the true number of seconds till the schedule. I'd prefer it if parse-crontab would do this.

issue_title: Overridden close() not called under windows
issue_body:
Hi, 
I have encountered a difference between Windows and Linux.

In Linux, if I close the connection from the server, the close()-function is called, and reason and other stuff are nicely presented to the client.

In Windows, only the closed(self, code, reason=None)-function is called, and not the close()-function.
I rather get the "ConnectionResetError: [WinError 10054] An existing connection was forcibly closed by the remote host".

The above error *may* be because the first close doesn't happen property and it is because the server is shutting down. But if so, the same thing would happen under Linux. It is kind of hard to say, as it is multiprocessing and so forth, so the order of logs differ depending on how quickly stuff happens on each platform.

It is completely possible that I am missing something, of course, but still..

Since `User.is_authenticated` is now a property in Django 2.x, the `is_authenticated()` call in `templatetags/adminlte_helpers.py` now raises an exception. There's PR #11 that removes the parentheses but it would break compatibility with <2.x, so something like

    user = context['request'].user if user is None else user
    authenticated = user.is_authenticated
    authenticated = authenticated() if callable(authenticated) else authenticated
    return 'https://www.gravatar.com/avatar/{hash}?s={size}&d=mm'.format(
        hash=md5(user.email.encode('utf-8')).hexdigest() if authenticated else '',
        size=size or '',
    )

should do the trick.

there's an issue with the command for migration if there's a lot of Session data and there's are Session close to expiration.

For example if a certain Session expires in 5 seconds before the command is ran, and then the migration runs for 20 seconds and hits this Session, the expire_in becomes 15 seconds:

```python
expire_in = session.expire_date - now
expire_in = expire_in.seconds + expire_in.days * 86400
```

it shouldn't be 15 seconds, in fact the Session should just be ignored.  I caught this error since you can't set expiration time 0 and I had a Session that expired right when the code hit it.

issue_title: Sorting succeeds on StackedInlines, but fails on TabularInlines
issue_body:
Hi there,

I have encountered the following bug with adminsortable version 2.0.12 and Django 1.9.2. When using a SortableTabularInline admin, the form rows can be dragged and dropped but the Ajax POST requests always returns the following response:

    {objects_sorted: false}

Hence, when reloading the page the objects are again in their original order. For debugging purposes I have changed [line 307 of admin.py](https://github.com/iambrandontaylor/django-admin-sortable/blob/master/adminsortable/admin.py#L307) from `pass` into `raise`, and then the POST request returns the following error:

    Exception Type: ValueError at /admin/autodidact/assignment/sorting/do-sorting/16/
    Exception Value: invalid literal for int() with base 10: ''
    Request information:
    GET: No GET data
    POST:
    indexes = u''

It seems that the variable `indexes` is empty, and that's why the sorting fails. I tried stepping through the Javascript code but I couldn't find a reason why `indexes` could be empty. This problem only occurs with SortableStackedInline. If I change my own `admin.py` to SortableStackedInline the sorting succeeds every time.

issue_title: Document.get_attachment() fails with binary data and when write_to is not None
issue_body:
Using 2.0.0b2 with Python 3.5.0 and connecting to CouchDB 1.6.1.

I'm trying to access an attachment with this code:

```python
image = Document(database, result['id'])
image_filename = '{0}.jpg'.format(image['_id'])

with open(image_filename, 'wb') as file:
    image.get_attachment('image', write_to=file, attachment_type='binary')
```

This produces the following exception:

```
  File "[redacted]/src/python/lemonaid.py", line 97, in seedannotations
    image.get_attachment('image', write_to=file, attachment_type='binary')
  File "[redacted]/lib/python3.5/site-packages/cloudant/document.py", line 396, in get_attachment
    write_to.write(resp.raw)
TypeError: a bytes-like object is required, not 'HTTPResponse'
```
In the debugger, the type of `resp.raw` is reported as `<class 'requests.packages.urllib3.response.HTTPResponse'>`.

Also, passing `binary` for `attachment_type` is not supported as documented. The `get_attachment()` method attempts to convert binary data to Unicode:

```python
# from get_attachment...
        if attachment_type == 'json':
            return resp.json()
        return unicode_(resp.content)
```

When the response is binary, the last line above produces this exception:

```
  File "[redacted]/python3.5/site-packages/cloudant/document.py", line 400, in get_attachment
    return unicode_(resp.content)
  File "[redacted]/lib/python3.5/site-packages/cloudant/_2to3.py", line 95, in unicode_
    return astr.decode(ENCODING) if hasattr(astr, 'decode') else astr
UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf-8' codec can't decode byte 0xff in position 0: invalid start byte
```

I would also argue that the `attachment_type` argument is ill-conceived. What if the caller does not know whether the attachment is binary or text. Ideally, the method should expose the content-type of the attachment rather than presume the caller knows. (In my case, my image attachment could be either PNG or JPEG, but since get_attachment() does not provide the content-type I'm guessing in my code right now.)

issue_title: Figure missing on MXNET System Architecture page
issue_body:
## Description
The input/output semantics figure is missing on MXNet System Architecture page. The following is currently shown:

[input/output semantics figure]

Page link: https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/architecture/overview.html

Is there any plan or interest in promoting Gluon to the core API so that it is accessible from languages other than Python? It would be a shame for functionality like Gluon's RNN support to be limited to the Python API.

TensorFlow has implemented a lot of important functionality only in the Python API rather than the core C/C++ API and I would hate to see MXNet go that route too. I think the cross language support is a huge selling point for MXNet.

Thoughts?

issue_title: sqldiff strips size from character varying under Postgres
issue_body:
One of my favorite features of django-extensions is the sqldiff command. It's really useful when working through development of django projects. But we've discovered a small "glitch" when used with Postgres.

model file declared thusly:

    myfield = models.CharField(max_length=8)

Becomes this in Postgres:

        Column |     Type                      |
    ---------------+---------------------------------+
        myfield  |  character varying(9)   |

BUT sqldiff returns:

    ALTER TABLE "mytable"
               ALTER "myfield" TYPE VARCHAR;

notice -- sqldiff wants to strip the size from the varchar declaration.

I understand it's a completely harmless suggestion from sqldiff (postgres actually draws no distinction between the two scheme declarations).  BUT -- we prefer having the size field preserved as a form of documentation in the schema.

We're somewhat familiar with the django-extensions code base but we're still a bit new to it -- and it does some pretty sophisticated things (cool -- but sophisticated). Any suggestions on where in the code we could focus our attention to apply our own "patch" would be greatly appreciated!

Thanks
Jim

issue_title: Develop best pratice of working with nested bundles
issue_body:
Right now, if I'd create my own bundle, and depend on the plone bundle, for example,

issue_title: django-libsass should apply CssAbsoluteFilter itself, in case it's disabled at the django-compressor level
issue_body:
Related: https://github.com/django-compressor/django-compressor/issues/226

When precompilers (such as django-libsass) are in use but compression is disabled, files are served from `/static/CACHE/` but django-compressor's CssAbsoluteFilter does not run. As a result, relative paths within the CSS are treated differently depending on whether or not compression is active.

In the absence of a solution to this problem at the django-compressor level, we can work around this by running CssAbsoluteFilter as part of the precompilation step, as described here: https://github.com/django-compressor/django-compressor/issues/226#issuecomment-57859011. I propose to incorporate this workaround into django-libsass itself.

It doesn't matter if django-compressor subsequently runs CssAbsoluteFilter again, because CssAbsoluteFilter leaves existing absolute paths intact - however, it will add a cache-busting suffix to them, so ideally we should run a monkey-patched version CssAbsoluteFilter that skips this step, to avoid this happening twice.

**cuda**: 9.0 cudnn: 7.0
**bazel**: 0.10.0

**Sys** : ubuntu 16.04
**board**: Jetson TX2

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
Hi all,

I had this error, when trying deepspeech after builds !

**POSSIBLE SOLUTION** 

For those who built Tensorflow from source, with a bazel version >= 5.0
you don't need the next Flag : **D_GLIBCXX_USE_CXX11_ABI=0**

so, you can build native client replacing : 

```
bazel build -c opt --copt=-O3 --copt="-D_GLIBCXX_USE_CXX11_ABI=0" //native_client:libctc_decoder_with_kenlm.so

bazel build --config=monolithic -c opt --copt=-O3 --copt="-D_GLIBCXX_USE_CXX11_ABI=0" --copt=-fvisibility=hidden //native_client:libdeepspeech.so //native_client:deepspeech_utils //native_client:generate_trie
```

by: 

```
bazel build -c opt --copt=-O3  //native_client:libctc_decoder_with_kenlm.so

bazel build --config=monolithic -c opt --copt=-O3 --copt=-fvisibility=hidden //native_client:libdeepspeech.so //native_client:deepspeech_utils //native_client:generate_trie
```

It worked for me.
Hope it will help each other.

Hello,

I have a threaded task that uses boto3 to copy files from an s3 bucket to another. Using `lsof` to view the connections I get one connection per thread. They first start with `(ESTABLISED)` then go to `(CLOSED_WAIT)` stay there for a minute and finally `(CLOSED)`. 

```
~ lsof | grep amazon
python    2951 alejandrodonascimiento   15u    IPv4 0x81b78f2f942da5fb        0t0      TCP 192.168.81.5:49766->s3-1-w.amazonaws.com:https (CLOSED)
python    2951 alejandrodonascimiento   15u    IPv4 0x81b78f2f942da5fb        0t0      TCP 192.168.81.5:49766->s3-1-w.amazonaws.com:https (CLOSED)
python    2951 alejandrodonascimiento   15u    IPv4 0x81b78f2f942da5fb        0t0      TCP 192.168.81.5:49766->s3-1-w.amazonaws.com:https (CLOSED)
...
```

The connections disappear when the process that executes the task terminates, the thing is that this is going to be executed as an async celery tasks, so the process should "never" terminate. 

If the tasks keeps executing on the same process it reuses the connection, but if the celery concurrency level is set to more than one and the tasks executes on different process, it once again opens one connection per thread, leaving the system with `CELERY_CONCURRENCY * NUM_THREADS` connections with `(CLOSED)` status.

For my use case I don't think leaving the connections is going to be an issue, I just want to know if I'm doing something wrong or if there is an explicit way of cleaning the connection pool.

Here is the code, that executes the task, what is called is the `deploy to production` method:

```python
class Deployer(object): 

    NUM_WORKERS_THREADS = 10

    def _s3_keys(self):
        """
        Generator returns the s3 keys copy.
        """ 
        ...

    def deploy_to_production(self):
        """
        Copy the files from one bucket to another
        """
        logger.debug('Starting deploy')
        q = Queue()
        threads = []
        for i in xrange(self.NUM_WORKERS_THREADS):
            t = Worker(q, i)
            t.start()
            threads.append(t)

        for key in self._s3_keys():
            q.put(key)

        for i in xrange(self.NUM_WORKERS_THREADS):
            q.put(None)

        q.join()
        logger.debug('Finished deploy')

class Worker(threading.Thread):

    DEST_BUCKET = '*******'
    SRC_BUCKET = '*******'

    def __init__(self, queue, i):
        threading.Thread.__init__(self)
        self.queue = queue
        self.id = i
        logger.debug('Init thread {}'.format(self.id))

    def run(self):
        """
        Connects to s3 and copy the keys consumed in the queue from the
        SRC_BUCKET to the DEST_BUCKET
        """
        session = boto3.session.Session(
            region_name='*******',
            aws_access_key_id='*******',
            aws_secret_access_key='*******'
        )

        s3 = session.resource('s3')
        while True:
            key_name = self.queue.get()
            if key_name is None:
                break
            s3.Object(self.DEST_BUCKET, key_name).copy_from(
                CopySource='{}/{}'.format(self.SRC_BUCKET, key_name)
            )
            self.queue.task_done()
        logger.debug('Closing thread {}'.format(self.id))
        self.queue.task_done()
```

Thanks

issue_title: Which is the best approach: have an IGNORE_INDEXES or an AVAILABLE_INDEXES in config.py?
issue_body:
As of now, just installing Products.PloneKeywordManager in a plone 4.3.9 instance we have two Keyword Fields: "Subject" and "commentators".

![selecao_184](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/8203476/16565440/862a0e0c-41e3-11e6-8b1f-f183217c0b41.png)

The way the logic is made right now with the tool it's using an [IGNORE_INDEXES](https://github.com/collective/Products.PloneKeywordManager/blob/7631104bd6a477485a2a067360ca9be944b2b854/Products/PloneKeywordManager/config.py#L12), so "commentators" should be added to it as well since it's not exactly a keyword we're going to edit or merge on a particular website.

So, if in a future version of Plone another index is added, this constant will need to be upgraded *again*.

We were thinking, wouldn't it be a better approach to have an AVAILABLE_INDEXES that would have only `Subject` - and if an **specific** policy would like to use this plugin to manage other KeywordIndexes, you could just patch it, instead of the implementation we have today? The real bonus would be to change this constant to a registry.xml configuration so tou don't even need a patch :)

Running tests against remote servers is generally a bad idea. Besides them being unreliable (failing randomly due to service outages or network problems), the user may be running a byte-counted data plan in which the tests unnecessarily increase his expenditures and cause privacy issues. We have those reasons enumerated in [Gentoo developer manual](https://devmanual.gentoo.org/ebuild-writing/functions/src_test/index.html#tests-that-require-network-or-service-access).

Please make it easily possible to disable the tests requiring network access, or possibly replace them with variants that work locally.

issue_title: IndexFile.remove: shell command length limit might be hit
issue_body:
On windows, there can be a command line length overflow as the shell might be used there to make the git command call as we pass the paths directly as argv. This is as we use git-rm to be able to remove whole directories easily. 

Alternatively, the index could be manipulated in-python or using git-update-index, and the working tree adjustments would have to be hand implemented then which might be some work to get it right ( and well tested ).

```
traceData = self.bHandle.data_arrays[traceName][int(traceStart*Fs):int(traceEnd*Fs)]

  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/nixio/pycore/data_array.py", line 28, in _read_data

  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/nixio/pycore/h5dataset.py", line 57, in read_data

  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/h5py/_hl/dataset.py", line 656, in read_direct

    raise TypeError("Can't broadcast %s -> %s" % (target_shape, count))
TypeError: Can't broadcast (97080,) -> (97081,)
```
Nixpy does not throw an exception when the index in a data_array call exceeds the number of items in the data_array. Instead it causes an error in h5py when the number of items retrieved from file does not match the number of expected items.
Maybe nixpy could instead check if the index is out of range before calling h5py.

issue_title: Terminate Function Seem Not Working Properly With SLURM
issue_body:
This issue only matters when using Vasp Monitors and only when running jobs in a SLURM environmental. It seems to originate from the Popen.terminate() python API. If a job terminates normally, all the function works properly. However, once a VASP job is killed by Popen.terminate(), which would be true if the Monitor finds an error, new VASP job will never be able to run. The error message is "Unable to create job step: Job/step already completing or completed". The power of custodian is **fully prohibited** in this situation.

How do you know whether you are affected? If the success rate of recent calculation degrades, probably you are already affected.

I reproduced the error using a simple python script without custodian and asked NERSC staff to fix it. They confirmed the issue and promised to investigate it. However, it has already been two weeks I didn't get any update. As a precaution, I think we should consider the possibility they are not able fix it in short term.

I have an alternative to walk-around this issue in my fork. The point is to use "scancel" to kill VASP job. This method releases the resource successfully while Popen.terminate() doesn't. It seems to be working fine for the last two weeks. However, it makes the custodian code more complicated and environment dependent. I am not sure whether it is a good idea to merge this piece of code to the main repository.

Probably refactor all instances of `warn` to instead use ``skimage._shared.warn`` and then implement it to always use `stacklevel=2`.

Also see http://permalink.gmane.org/gmane.comp.python.numeric.general/62455

issue_title: Implement negative log-likelihood as an optional error metric
issue_body:
@chetan51  @subutai 
All the existing error metrics consider only the single best prediction from the CLAModel. In scenarios where multiple predictions are possible, it is more appropriate to use the negative log-likelihood as the error metrics. Given the predicted probability of all possible outcomes, likelihood is the predicted probability of the actual outcome. The log-likelihood is an additive metric and can be accumulated like many of the existing error metrics. 

A recent example of using likelihood as the error metric can be found at
http://arxiv.org/abs/1412.3555  (section 4.1)

Proposed changes
https://www.evernote.com/l/AZ3TtrOnOIhCYIMeLApI104iWlahr3ZM8hI

issue_title: OS X binaries don't work with Homebrew Python [OS X 10]
issue_body:
## Environment
- OS X 10.10
- Python: Homebrew Python 2.7.9

## To Reproduce

1. `pip uninstall nupic` to ensure no existing installation
1. `pip install nupic` (installs one of the binaries below)
1. `python -c "import nupic.bindings.math"`

### Error:

```
Fatal Python error: PyThreadState_Get: no current thread
[1]    77717 abort      python -c "import nupic.bindings.math"
```

I tried this manually by downloading both the wheels below and got the same result with either one:

- [nupic-0.1.2-cp27-none-macosx_10_9_intel.whl](https://pypi.python.org/packages/cp27/n/nupic/nupic-0.1.2-cp27-none-macosx_10_9_intel.whl#md5=d7843d31e9c6b96c8fc727e6ea0e672c)
- [nupic-0.1.2-cp27-none-macosx_10_10_intel.whl](https://pypi.python.org/packages/2.7/n/nupic/nupic-0.1.2-cp27-none-macosx_10_10_intel.whl#md5=3d9d57cd0a8ee9c795209a8b554b683a)

To install from one of these manually, just download them and `pip install path/to/wheel`.

## Potential Cause

Unpacking and inspecting the wheel file gives us a look into how the `_math.so` binary was built on Travis-CI:

```
∙ cp nupic-0.1.2-cp27-none-macosx_10_10_intel.whl nupic-0.1.2-cp27-none-macosx_10_10_intel.zip
∙ unzip nupic-0.1.2-cp27-none-macosx_10_10_intel.zip
∙ cd nupic-0.1.2-cp27-none-macosx_10_10_intel
∙ find . -name _math.so
./nupic/bindings/_math.so
∙ otool -L nupic/bindings/_math.so
nupic/bindings/_math.so:
	/usr/lib/libSystem.B.dylib (compatibility version 1.0.0, current version 1213.0.0)
	/System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/Python (compatibility version 2.7.0, current version 2.7.6)
	/usr/lib/libc++.1.dylib (compatibility version 1.0.0, current version 120.0.0)
```

So `_math.so` was built on Travis-CI using the local system python as a shared library. Because `/System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/Python` exists on my local system as the default Python implementation, there is a mismatch in python versions when using this file.

As a comparison, I wanted to `otool` the `_math.so` file that gets generated locally when I build NuPIC from source, so after running a build locally using homebrew python I ran `otool` on the locally generated `_math.so` file:

```
∙ otool -L nupic/bindings/_math.so
nupic/bindings/_math.so:
	/usr/lib/libSystem.B.dylib (compatibility version 1.0.0, current version 1213.0.0)
	/usr/local/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/Python (compatibility version 2.7.0, current version 2.7.0)
	/usr/local/lib/libkj-0.6-dev.dylib (compatibility version 0.0.0, current version 0.0.0)
	/usr/local/lib/libcapnp-0.6-dev.dylib (compatibility version 0.0.0, current version 0.0.0)
	/usr/local/lib/libcapnpc-0.6-dev.dylib (compatibility version 0.0.0, current version 0.0.0)
	/usr/lib/libc++.1.dylib (compatibility version 1.0.0, current version 120.0.0)
```

This works fine with homebrew Python because `/usr/local/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/Python` soft links to `/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.9/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/Python`.

issue_title: CoordinateEncoder not compatible with float radii
issue_body:
The docstrings for `CoordinateEncoder._neighbors()` and `CoordinateEncoder.encodeIntoArray()` specify `float` values for radius, but using floats result in exceptions.  This is due to an implementation detail in `_neighbors()` where radius is added to values in the coordinate, forcing a cast to float in the second argument of `range()`.  For example, the following code snippet produces a `TypeError` exception:

```
CoordinateEncoder().encode((numpy.array([1]), 3.0))
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/Users/amarshall/nta/nupic/src/nupic/encoders/base.py", line 133, in encode
    self.encodeIntoArray(inputData, output)
  File "/Users/amarshall/nta/nupic/src/nupic/encoders/coordinate.py", line 109, in encodeIntoArray
    neighbors = self._neighbors(coordinate, radius)
  File "/Users/amarshall/nta/nupic/src/nupic/encoders/coordinate.py", line 130, in _neighbors
    ranges = [range(n-radius, n+radius+1) for n in coordinate.tolist()]
TypeError: range() integer end argument expected, got float.
```

I think corrective action should be to fix documentation to reflect the correct type and possibly assert on type in `CoordinateEncoder.encodeIntoArray()`

issue_body:
see https://github.com/mozilla-services/cliquet/blob/master/cliquet/views/hello.py#L14

it seems to be clearer to rename it to project_name

In Kinto, we usually include the authentication package because it can register a couple of things like a heartbeat ping or a *capability*, that allows client to figure out that a policy is enabled.

But since we also rely on the policy name declared in `pyramid_multiauth` to add a prefix to the user id (eg. `portier:contact@me.com`, `github:leplatrem`), we want a nice name and not the package name (eg. `kinto_portier` or `kinto.plugins.github`). Therefore we specify a name and the `policy.use` setting.

My suggestion would be to introduce `policy.include` that would allow to replace that:

```ini
pyramid.includes = kinto_portier

multiauth.policies = portier
multiauth.policy.portier.use = kinto_portier.authentication.PortierOAuthAuthenticationPolicy
```

with just that:
```ini
multiauth.policies = portier
multiauth.policy.portier.include = kinto_portier
```

We could also keep the `use` setting and introspect the target, but explicit is better than...you know.

What do you think?

Hey Browserstack folks,

Not sure if you realized this, but when you pushed this package to PyPi, you clobbered an existing package name, [browserstack-local](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/browserstack-local/0.2). You also used a version signifying that you were making a backwards-compatible change to the existing package (see [semver](http://semver.org/)). Not only is this an entirely different package, it's also incompatible with the one already released (e.g. different namespaces; different API).

I think it's generally not good etiquette to clobber packages in PyPi, even if the purpose is similar. It took us half a day to track down our failing integration tests to this release.

I would encourage you to either pick another package name, or do a major version bump (e.g. 1.0) to indicate that this is a backwards incompatible change.

Thank you.

issue_title: Inconsistency in controller handling for multi-class run
issue_body:
In a test run with multiple classes (like a suite), the controller objects are created and destroyed for every class. But the controller info is only dumped once at the very end of the test as a test run level block.

Recording class-level info as a run-level info causes information loss and inconsistency in reporting.

E.g. we allow users to add custom info to `AndroidDevice` obj in test. Right now if two classes are executed and they add different custom info to their `AndroidDevice` objects, some info is lost.

The root cause comes from this comment
https://github.com/google/mobly/blob/811e52a95fd8c35253dbd918cd7fcbda8a78369b/mobly/records.py#L490

The controller info is **not** the same across all classes in the same run, even if the same controller units are used in reality.
Change it to .b please

issue_title: we should enable data page checksums
issue_body:
If patroni is initdb'ing, we should enable data page checksums because:

* it's a good idea
* performance impact is not noticeable under most circumstances
* prior to 9.5, it's required for pg_rewind to be safe to use

Hello there,

I've been finding the need to store irods sessions inside a `postgresql blob`, and looked up how to make them be as binary strings.

I've seen that python uses the `pickle` library for this. So I've been trying:

```python
$ ipython

In [1]: from irods.session import iRODSSession

In [2]: sess = iRODSSession(...)

In [3]: sess.users.get('guest')
Out[3]: <iRODSUser 10019 guest rodsuser tempZone>

In [4]: import pickle

In [5]: pickle.dumps(sess)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-6-a81a58406cef> in <module>()
----> 1 pickle.dumps(sess)

TypeError: can't pickle _thread.lock objects
```

So I've been digging this and it seems that the `Pool` irods class stores a lock for threads that blocks the *serialization*. Do we want to fix this?

I can create a pull request to update the session class adding private methods`__getstate__` and `__setstate__` (see the [docs](https://docs.python.org/2/library/pickle.html#object.__getstate__)). Would it be fine? Should this a new child class or would that be applied to the existing one?

Finally unittests should do `dumps` and then `load` from pickle without errors.

issue_title: Provide an endpoint to query versions of core and loaded packages
issue_body:
Consider a developer running a service against a local instance of OpenFisca (for instance because they want to minimize the network path, or because they've enabled a computationally expensive reform) wanting to quickly know whether upgrading to the latest version breaks anything.

They might connect their service to api.openfisca.fr for testing purposes, and decide to upgrade if nothing seems to break (and their CI tests pass, etc.). But at present there doesn't seem to be a way to know what versions are running on api.openfisca.fr and thus to know just what it is safe to upgrade to.

The API itself returns a version number but that is very coarse-grained; the X-API-Version header returns the version number of this repository, but that doesn't seem quite reliable as a way of knowing the exact versions of the underlying core, packages and reforms.

A `/version` endpoint returning the version numbers of openfisca-core and any packages loaded into OpenFisca (country, extensions, maybe even enabled reforms) would be valuable in this and possibly other scenarios. Alternately, if I've missed an existing way to check this, I'd love to learn of it!

issue_title: datastore: No way to exclude_from_indexes new properties on updated entities
issue_body:
When loading an `Entity` from the datastore and updating it, there is no way to exclude new attributes from being indexed, since the only public API for setting `exclude_from_indexes` is on the `Entity` constructor. To work around this, I am currently setting `entity._exclude_from_indexes = (...)` in my code, which is not a "public" API.

Example:

```python
client = google.cloud.datastore.Client()
key = client.key('SomeEntityKey')
entity = google.cloud.datastore.Entity(key, exclude_from_indexes=('foo', 'bar'))
entity['foo'] = 'foo'
print 'entity.exclude_from_indexes:', entity.exclude_from_indexes
client.put(entity)

entity2 = client.get(entity.key)
print 'entity2.exclude_from_indexes:', entity2.exclude_from_indexes
entity2['bar'] = 'bar'
client.put(entity2)
```

Output:

```
entity.exclude_from_indexes: frozenset(['foo', 'bar'])
entity2.exclude_from_indexes: frozenset([u'foo'])
```

This is actually "expected" based on how the Datastore works, however, in the code sample above, there should be some way for me to put the entity back, without copying it into a brand new `Entity`.


Tested using:

* Mac OS X 10.11.6
* Python 2.7.10
* The following Google packages:
```
gapic-google-cloud-datastore-v1==0.15.3
google-auth==1.0.1

googleapis-common-protos==1.5.2

I am trying use `bootstrap-sass` via `npm`, `node-sass` and the `sass-module-importer`. The importer [documents](https://www.npmjs.com/package/sass-module-importer) this lookup strategy:

    1. Set a SCSS/Sass/CSS file on the "main" field of their package.json/bower.json
    2. Set a SCSS/Sass/CSS file on the "style" field of their package.json/bower.json
    3. Have a index.css file on the root of their module

If I look at the `bootstrap` npm package, [it uses](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/7c86bc9664ddc3a1965fe91d7a0db97eb9348448/package.json#L21) the `style` field in `package.json`. It seems [this field is becoming a de facto standard](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/32037150/style-field-in-package-json).

Is it possible to get a similar setup and expose the main scss file in the `style` field of `package.json`?

If one use `nohup python autoban.py &>/var/log/autoban.log &`, then IPs and ban commands will not immediatly appear in autoban.log as they happens due to output buffering of sys.stdout and sys.stderr. This is not desirable for monitoring and dignostic purpose.

In python3, this can be resolved with `print(msg, flush=True)`, but a custom print fuction might be needed for python2 compatibility.

[root@default ~]# pip install shadowsocks
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/bin/pip", line 7, in ?

  File "/usr/lib/python2.4/site-packages/pkg_resources.py", line 236, in load_entry_point
    return get_distribution(dist).load_entry_point(group, name)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.4/site-packages/pkg_resources.py", line 2097, in load_entry_point
    return ep.load()
  File "/usr/lib/python2.4/site-packages/pkg_resources.py", line 1830, in load
    entry = __import__(self.module_name, globals(),globals(), ['__name__'])
  File "/usr/lib/python2.4/site-packages/pip-7.1.0-py2.4.egg/pip/__init__.py", line 211
    except PipError as exc:
                     ^
SyntaxError: invalid syntax

The autoscaling.py file is missing the following lines:

```
AttachLoadBalancers = Action(prefix, 'AttachLoadBalancers'),
DetachLoadBalancers = Action(prefix, 'DetachLoadBalancers')
```

These were actually missing from the AWS console until recently due to an oversight on their end. They have always been valid permissions, but just didn't exist in the "IAM Role Generator" UI. I filed a support request with AWS and they added them.

https://github.com/cloudtools/troposphere/blob/87d254502a6cc99560598ba2d8c9c7ff8aa366c0/troposphere/firehose.py#L147

Is there support for "ProcessingConfiguration"
http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-properties-kinesisfirehose-deliverystream-processingconfiguration.html

issue_title: Preserve PEX_ env variables after calling pex
issue_body:
https://github.com/pantsbuild/pex/blob/master/pex/pex.py#L48

Currently we delete all env variables from os.environ that start with PEX_.  If pex is called from another process as a library this will override the parent scripts env variables.  If the env variables are reset back to their original state at the end of execution the behavior would be less confusing when using pex as a library.

For example in pants if we try to override the PEX_ROOT every time we call pex to build a target PEX_ROOT is reset.

issue_title: Namespace packages for python tests cannot be combined.
issue_body:
See https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/1147 - in particular, travis run https://travis-ci.org/pantsbuild/pants/builds/51593758.

This run has a single failing shard: https://travis-ci.org/pantsbuild/pants/jobs/51593762
```console
============== test session starts ===============
                     platform linux2 -- Python 2.7.8 -- py-1.4.26 -- pytest-2.6.4
                     plugins: cov, timeout
                     collected 7 items / 2 errors 
                     
                     contrib/cpp/tests/python/pants_test/contrib/cpp/test_cpp_integration.py ...
                     contrib/cpp/tests/python/pants_test/contrib/cpp/test_cpp_toolchain.py ....
                     
                     ===================== ERRORS =====================
                      ERROR collecting contrib/scrooge/tests/python/pants_test/contrib/scrooge/tasks/test_scrooge_gen.py 
                     /tmp/tmpbRsRoP/.deps/py-1.4.26-py2.py3-none-any.whl/py/_path/local.py:641: in pyimport
                         __import__(modname)
                     E   ImportError: No module named scrooge.tasks.test_scrooge_gen
                      ERROR collecting contrib/scrooge/tests/python/pants_test/contrib/scrooge/tasks/test_thrift_linter_integration.py 
                     /tmp/tmpbRsRoP/.deps/py-1.4.26-py2.py3-none-any.whl/py/_path/local.py:641: in pyimport
                         __import__(modname)
                     E   ImportError: No module named scrooge.tasks.test_thrift_linter_integration
                     ======= 7 passed, 2 error in 4.57 seconds ========

FAILURE


               FAILURE
```

The key thing to note is both `contrib/cpp` and `contrib/scrooge` tests contributing to the `pants_test/contrib/...` namespace are in-play in a single test_runner chroot.  If the tests each get their own chroot (`--no-fast`), the problem goes away.  It would be nice to be able to use the default `--fast` mode if just to understand the test_runner ns package issue for test code.

It has become obvious that our current invalidation granularity (the python function level) is too fine-grained to implement filesystem access efficiently. There seem to be a very good supply of rust libraries to support this type of filesystem access.

Two related improvements would then be to either:
1. provide support for writing any Task function in rust instead (very general, but potentially overkill), and then porting most of {{fs.py}} to rust.
1. port {{fs.py}} directly to rust as intrinisics, with a reduced public API (ie, one that only supports generating Snapshot objects for PathGlobs).

The former choice seems more general, but I would be concerned that the invalidation tracking overhead would remain a bottleneck, so I'm leaning toward option 2.

issue_title: Implement fail-slow for the local engines
issue_body:
This is a demonstration of the solution to #915 which can be done easily now.
Each engine keeps a mapping of promise to product returned by the plan executor.  Just need to trap raise from the executor and if fail slow, associate a poision product with the promise.  Then when calling plan.bind going forward in following plan executions, we can check if the binding has grabbed any poisoned products and if so, short circuit that particular execution.

issue_title: CNN Decoder only supports a maximum length of 128, but 130 was requested....
issue_body:
Hi! When I try to train (and translate with) a CNN encoder & decoder, I constantly get a maximum length error like

> sockeye.utils.SockeyeError: Decoder only supports a maximum length of 128, but 130 was requested. Note that the maximum output length depends on the input length and the source/target length ratio observed during training.

I did get it to work when specifying a small enough length
```
python -m sockeye.translate --models . \
                            --use-cpu \
                            --max-input-len 97 \
                            < decode.source \
                            > decode.output
```

Training parameters I used:
```
python -m sockeye.train --prepared-data data-prepared \
                        -vs newsdev2017.bpe.en \
                        -vt newsdev2017.bpe.lv \
                        --batch-size 128 \
                        --num-embed 512 \
                        --decode-and-evaluate -1 \
                        --checkpoint-frequency 3000 \
                        --use-tensorboard \
                        -o wmt_conv_model \
                        --encoder cnn \
                        --decoder cnn \
                        --num-layers 6:6 \
                        --max-seq-len 128
```

issue_title: Adding "+" to the version in development
issue_body:
I want to add a new convention to our development process. I think we should denote the version in the development archive with whatever version was released last with a "+" suffixed.

That will make it easier to deduce from the version string alone, what kind of installation a user that reports an issue has.

I already changed the string the __init__.py file and unless someone objects wildly I will update the "how to do a release" section of the docs.

issue_title: Server should not be killed if one request has any issues
issue_body:
This `sys.exit()` call in the replay is dangerous. If it gets hit dues to a a request falling into an unrecognized exception, the whole replay server daemon will die. This should just log the exception, return a, `HTTP 500 Internal Server Error` and move one.

https://github.com/oduwsdl/ipwb/blob/f870b242e895379eabdb427ae5726e3e12eead51/ipwb/replay.py#L620

Other two uses of the `sys.exit()` in the same script are fine as they will only occur at the startup, not when the server is up and serving.

issue_title: Redirect to serviceWorker installation if first access is a URI-M
issue_body:
Related to #157 where a client sends the version of the serviceWorker they have installed. If a client's first-access is not the main (root) ipwb page, the serviceWorker will not be installed. This is because the scope needs to be set and having a nested URI to set scope limits the allowed scopes relative to that URI.

On replaying a "root memento/URI-M", check if the serviceWorker is installed (and optionally, current per #157). Redirect or perform some action to get this installed so URI-M rerouting (cf. rewriting) works correctly via the serviceWorker.

NOTE: This could break curling if we go the server-side issued HTTP 3XX route. For example, if a user wanted to just get the root HTML page of the URI-M, a 3XX would prevent this.

issue_title: Documentation: Expected Value Format for "One of"
Apply Engineering and Pin Versions of Required Libraries
issue_body:
You should explicitly list all required libraries, including `requests`, here:

https://github.com/AuthorizeNet/sdk-python/blob/e2c7c9ebcd97b8e225245886588b306e17ddcffc/authorizenet/apicontrollersbase.py#L11

And you are being negligent by not specifying versions of each library you use.  If you do not pin versions of libraries, it is unclear what your test cases actually mean when they passed---we do not know what versions of libraries your software works with.

Since commits are intermittent on this project, I highly recommend you pin the versions so that we don't have to worry about grabbing a new version that breaks your library---and in the worst case silently.

issue_title: Overhaul the pyiso README to be more readable.
issue_body:
Currently, pyiso's README could be summed up as containing the following content:

1. relevant badges giving newcomers a glance at the project's overall state of health.
2. a two-sentence description of what the project is for.
3. a link to the full documentation on Read The Docs.
4. a link back to the guiding organization, WattTime.
5. a changelog.

The first four items are a good start, but could use some improvement. For item five, the changelog, I'd be more inclined to look at https://github.com/WattTime/pyiso/releases.

All that said, I think pyiso's README could use a hug. While doing some background reading on the topic, many site point to https://open-source-guide.18f.gov/making-readmes-readable/ as a good point of reference. I agree, that if we follow the guidance in that article, our README would be much improved. Also notable is someone's list of exemplar project README documents, https://github.com/matiassingers/awesome-readme.

To fix this issue, I think the following changes should be made:
1. Modify release information in each release listed in https://github.com/WattTime/pyiso/releases to contain the current Change Log contents 
OR
Break the changelog out into its own CHANGELOG.md file.
2. Modify our README content to conform to the guidance provided in https://open-source-guide.18f.gov/making-readmes-readable/ to maximize its utility.
3. Look over some examples from https://github.com/matiassingers/awesome-readme and make some final modifications so that it draws new contributors in (i.e. a picture is worth a thousand words and logos give a sense of legitimacy).

issue_title: ValueError: X must contain at least two samples.

issue_body:
### What is wrong?
See https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/pull/273#discussion_r161866203
In `ExecutionContext` (evm.vm.execution_context) and `TransactionContext` (#327), it's better to write an explicit decorator `read_only_property` or `immutable_property` which includes a setter that raises such an `AttributeError` when someone try to modify these properties.

### How can it be fixed
1. Write the decorator in [eth-utils](https://github.com/ethereum/eth-utils) or a new file `evm/utils/decorators.py`
2. Update the decorator of `ExecutionContext` and `TransactionContext`

issue_title: WARNING: The remote server could not find the noarch directory for the requested channel with url: https://conda.anaconda.org/conda-default
issue_body:
Forgive me because I am still new to this, but I can't create new conda environments and do not know why.  I am running Python 2.7 in anaconda and am trying to run python 3.5 so I can run Tensorflow, according to these instructions: https://conda.io/docs/py2or3.html


However when I enter conda create -n py35 python=3.5 anaconda, I get the following:

Fetching package metadata ....
WARNING: The remote server could not find the noarch directory for the
requested channel with url: https://conda.anaconda.org/conda-default

It is possible you have given conda an invalid channel. Please double-check
your conda configuration using `conda config --show`.

If the requested url is in fact a valid conda channel, please request that the
channel administrator create `noarch/repodata.json` and associated
`noarch/repodata.json.bz2` files, even if `noarch/repodata.json` is empty.
$ mkdir noarch
$ echo '{}' > noarch/repodata.json
$ bzip2 -k noarch/repodata.json
...


PackageNotFoundError: Package missing in current win-64 channels:
  - python 3.5*


I have been searching for hours and have no idea what is causing this.

Apart from conda itself, this also concerns menuinst (ping @ilanschnell ).
Do you think it will be possible/worthwhile to provide a way to include `menuinst`-menus inside a `noarch`-package?
A use case would be an cross-platform application which could be perfectly packaged with `noarch: ...` -- but also happens to have a GUI for which Windows users would benefit from having a start menu entry/desktop link. Currently one needs to build platform-dependent packages for this or supply some additional helper package which provides those menus.
I see it is problematic since the `.json` (+`.ico`, ...) files are platform-dependent which kinda goes against `noarch`. If one wants to include them regardless, either conda or conda and menuinst need to be changed to support this. That is, in effect the copying step during package building (e.g., [console_shortcut/bld.bat](https://github.com/ContinuumIO/anaconda-recipes/blob/master/console_shortcut/bld.bat)) would most likely be postponed until the user installation (post-link step?) and the package could contain platform sub folders like `Menu/win` etc.

issue_title: new auxlib needs to call git subprocess with cwd set
issue_body: @kalefranz the new auxlib just gave me the strangest error, which took me a while to figure out.  The reason was because I was working on a project (constructor) which is also under git revision control.  After some debugging, I realized that calling `git` in the new `auxlib` is the problem.  You need to ensure that all subprocess calls to `git` use the `cwd` keyword argument (with value set to the conda root directory).  Otherwise `git` is called on whatever other project you happen to work on, and you get errors which are hard to debug.

issue_title: conda-4.4.0rc0 channel history can break conda
issue_body:
If the user has a private local repo or the Anaconda Repository and the hostname/ip changed or is no longer valid, conda will refuse to install any packages.

This happens even if you update your `.condarc` to only have the defaults channel again.

```
$ conda list --explicit

...
```
Note that one package was installed via a private repo that is no longer running from that IP address/hostname.

When attempting to install any other package from the defaults channel

```
$ conda install cx_oracle
Solving environment: failed

CondaHTTPError: HTTP 000 CONNECTION FAILED for url <http://10.200.31.60:8081/conda/anaconda/noarch/repodata.json>
Elapsed: -

An HTTP error occurred when trying to retrieve this URL.
HTTP errors are often intermittent, and a simple retry will get you on your way.
ConnectionError(MaxRetryError("HTTPConnectionPool(host='10.200.31.60', port=8081): Max retries exceeded with url: /conda/t/je-223eba05-93e2-42af-8024-606a6001e166/anaconda/noarch/repodata.json (Caused by NewConnectionError('<requests.packages.urllib3.connection.HTTPConnection object at 0x1031433c8>: Failed to establish a new connection: [Errno 61] Connection refused',))",),)
```

It appears that it is trying to contact the dead repo from that one package even if its no longer one of the channels in `.condarc`

Conda Config

```
channels:
  - defaults
ssl_verify: true
```

issue_title: Support of new MX firmware with dynamixel v2 protocol
issue_body:
Robotis released on March 3rd, 2017 the v39 of all MX motors, which introduce the usage of Dynamixel v2 protocol.

We should change dynamixel package to use Dxl320IO instead of DxlIO if firmware version is above 38.
We should also rename Dxl320IO Io to DxlIOv2 and DxlIO to DxlIOv1 but this will break the backward compatibility.

Before taking time to fix this issue (contribution is welcome), you should downgrade the firmware with Robotis ROBOPLUS tool.

Ressources: 
* [Robotis post](http://en.robotis.com/BlueAD/board.php?bbs_id=faq&mode=view&bbs_no=52) about that change.
* [Forum post](https://forum.poppy-project.org/t/mx-28-64-firmware-issues/3110/2) from Thot who spotted the issue
